user_query:
I’m standardizing how we categorize digitized historical records and need a small end-to-end update in our local workspace. Using the provided inputs, please do the following and save all outputs under the specified paths.

1) Enrich records and summarize:
- Read input/records.jsonl and input/category_mapping.csv.
- For each record, compute a standardized_category using a majority-of-tags rule: map each tag (case-insensitive) via input/category_mapping.csv, count mapped categories, and assign the category with the highest count. If there’s a tie, break it using the record’s existing category if it maps cleanly to one of the standardized categories via the same keyword mapping; otherwise set "Uncategorized". Normalize all tags to lowercase with duplicates removed in the output.
- Add a quality_flags array for each record with any of: missing_date (date is empty), missing_location (location is empty), missing_category (the original category field is empty).
- Write the enriched result to out/enriched_records.jsonl (JSON Lines with all original fields plus standardized_category and quality_flags).
- Write a category distribution summary to out/category_summary.csv with columns: standard_category,count (include only categories that appear in out/enriched_records.jsonl; exclude Uncategorized if it does not appear).
- Write a quality issues list to out/quality_issues.csv with columns: id,issues, where issues is a semicolon-delimited list of the flags present for that record.

2) Update the cataloging guidelines (edit in place):
- Open docs/cataloging_guidelines.md and update the top title/version from v1.0 to v1.1.
- Replace the "Categories" section to list the distinct standardized categories observed in out/enriched_records.jsonl (exclude Uncategorized if none). Do not add categories that do not appear in the enriched data.
- In the "Field Naming" section, update our names to: date and location (replacing references to record_date and place_name). Keep any other field notes intact.
- Add a new "Categorization Rules" section that documents: the majority-of-tags rule; the tie-break using the original category; and that any unmapped case results in "Uncategorized". Include one brief example referencing a record ID from input/records.jsonl to illustrate the rule (e.g., how tags led to the assigned standardized category).
- Append an entry to the existing "Change Log" for v1.1 summarizing these changes.
- Do not remove sections unrelated to these updates.

3) Draft an email to the county archivist:
- Read docs/email_context.md and use its "To:" line and bullet points.
- Create out/email_to_archivist.txt containing: a subject line; the recipient from the context; and a concise, professional body that includes: (a) counts per standardized_category derived from out/category_summary.csv; (b) the number of records with missing_date and missing_location derived from out/quality_issues.csv; (c) a list of the affected record IDs for each issue type; and (d) a "Questions" section that quotes the bullet points from docs/email_context.md verbatim.

4) Compose an internal status report:
- Write out/status_report.md with the following sections: Overview; Data Quality Summary; Category Distribution (a simple table or list consistent with out/category_summary.csv); Guideline Updates (summarize what changed in docs/cataloging_guidelines.md); Next Steps.
- All metrics in the report must match the values in the outputs you generated.

Constraints:
- Use only the provided local inputs. Do not invent records or categories beyond those derivable from input/category_mapping.csv and input/records.jsonl.
- All outputs must be deterministic given the inputs.
- Save outputs exactly to the specified paths and leave other files unchanged except for the in-place edits to docs/cataloging_guidelines.md. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
